Document:
# Crime Reference Tables
[color=lightblue][bold]OOC[/bold]: It is strongly recommended to increase the size of your guidebook window when viewing this page.[/color]
## Quick Reference Table
Code XX-I XX-II XX-III XX-IV XX-V
01-X Endangerment Mass Endangerment
02-X Assault Battery Maiming
03-X Manslaughter Suicide Murder Mass Murder Prevention of Revival
04-X Harassment
05-X Disorderly Conduct Mass Disturbance Rioting
06-X Obstruction
07-X Vandalism Tampering Destruction of Property Mass Destruction Facility Destruction
08-X Theft Grand Theft Vessel Hijack
09-X Neglect of Duty Breach of Procedure Abuse of Authority
10-X Possession: Substances Possession: Restricted Possession: Contraband
11-X Distribution: Substances Distribution: Restricted Distribution: Contraband
12-X Failure to Comply Resisting Arrest Breach of Custody
13-X Trespass Secure trespass
14-X Coercion Unlawful Control Hostage Taking
15-X Animal Abuse Animal Slaughter
16-X Bribery Fraud Forgery Impersonation
\n
## Extended Reference Table
Crime Description Max Sentencing (H:MM) Notes
Endangerment To recklessly put yourself or others in danger, either through direct action, or unreasonable failure to act. Confinement: 0:08 Covers things such as self experimentation, failure to act as a security officer, and operating small scale machines without the proper knowledge.
Mass Endangerment To recklessly put yourself and/or several others in danger, either through direct action or unreasonable failure to act. Confinement: 0:18 Similar to Endangerment but for larger scale things like operating station infrastructure without proper knowledge.
\n \n \n \n
Assault To threaten another person with physical harm or death. Confinement: 0:08 Can be extended even to personal spats and sometimes jokes if the receiving party was seriously frightened for their life.
Battery To physically harm another person without the intent to kill. Confinement: 0:14 Intent is the difference between battery and attempted murder.
Maiming To physically harm someone to the extent of causing extended damage to their being or impairment to their function. Confinement: 0:24 Ranges from severely damaged digits to dismemberment.
\n \n \n \n
Manslaughter To cause another person's death without the intent to kill, either through direct action or unreasonable failure to act. Confinement: 0:20 Includes manslaughter in self-defense so long as the threat could have been reasonably neutralized without the loss of life.
Suicide To cause your own death with intent. Confinement: 0:34, Psychiatric Custody A do not resuscitate order may also be preferable for repeated suicide while in custody.
Murder To cause another person's death with the intent to kill. Confinement: 0:46, Psychiatric Custody The difference between attempted murder and complete murder is if the victim ends up medically deceased and requires defibrillation for recovery.
Mass Murder To cause three or more people's deaths with the intent to kill. Confinement: 2:00, Permanent/Psychiatric Custody Intention still matters.
Prevention of Revival To render a corpse unrevivable through most standard means, either through direct action or unreasonable failure to act. Confinement: 2:30, Permanent/Psychiatric Custody, Execution Things such as ashing, gibbing, spacing, and rotting. Even covers accidental cases, however obviously with a much lighter sentence.
\n \n \n \n
Harassment To continuously interact with and bother another specific person after requests for you to stop. Confinement: 0:08 Does not cover someone performing their required duty in a polite manner.
\n \n \n \n
Disorderly Conduct To conduct one's self poorly to the extent of disturbing others. Confinement: 0:06 The catch all for most small scale hooliganism. Covers things such as public nudity, needless insults, and inciting other criminal activity.
Mass Disturbance To conduct one's self poorly to the extent of disturbing a significant number of others, such as a room full. Confinement: 0:12 Ranges from misuse of communication channels to bomb threats.
Rioting To take part in a large group of people causing mass disturbance or other disruptive activity. Confinement: 0:18 Leaders of a riot can be charged with any crime they may have incited during their leadership.
\n \n \n \n
Obstruction To prevent others from performing their duties through the use of physical blockades or refusing to move out of their way. Confinement: 0:08 Covers things such as making hallways difficult to traverse and getting overly close during a security arrest.
\n \n \n \n
Vandalism To deface or minorly damage property. Confinement: 0:06 For things that are easy to clean or repair like graffiti and small amounts of damage to windows.
Tampering To alter, bypass, or damage a machine or device's function. Confinement: 0:10 For things such as hacking vending machines, airlocks, telecommunication servers, and access cards.
Destruction of Property To completely destroy or cause a piece of property to be non-functional. Confinement: 0:14 For things that aren't so easy to clean or repair like breaking down walls.
Mass Destruction To cause destruction on a mass scale, rendering an entire area non-functional. Confinement: 0:30 For things that disable an entire area like damaging critical power, atmospheric, and hull structure.
Facility Destruction To cause the destruction or inhabitability of an entire facility or vessel. Confinement: 2:00, Permanent Custody Be it intentional like attempting to use nuclear weaponry or accidental like failing to contain a singularity.
\n \n \n \n
Theft To intentionally take an item that does not belong to you. Confinement: 0:08 Items such as tools, access cards, and apparel.
Grand Theft To intentionally take an item that does not belong to you and is virtually irreplaceable. Confinement: 0:16 Items such as door remotes, irreplaceable machines, and very personal belongings.
Vessel Hijack To intentionally take control and attempt to steal a shuttle, ship, or vessel that does not belong to you. Confinement: 0:28 Doesn't include vessels classified as derelict under Standard Operating Procedure.
\n \n \n \n
Neglect of Duty To fail to perform your job or duty to an unreasonable degree. Confinement: 0:08 Covers things such as failing to respond to an urgent job related matter. It may sometimes be better to seek demotion from their Head of Staff.
Breach of Procedure To violate the facility's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:12 Violating any measure of SOP that you're supposed to follow. It may sometimes be better to seek demotion from their Head of Staff.
Abuse of Authority To use your position of authority in order to engage in fraudulent behaviors, either unlawfully punishing others or covering up criminal activity. Confinement: 0:36 Includes things such as taking bribes, planting evidence, or using unlawful force. It may sometimes be better to seek demotion from their Head of Staff.
\n \n \n \n
Possession: Substances To unlawfully use or possess substances that are controlled under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:06 Covers anything under the Controlled Substances category in Standard Operating Procedure.
Possession: Restricted To unlawfully use or possess equipment or weaponry that is restricted under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:10 Covers anything under the Restricted Equipment category in Standard Operating Procedure.
Possession: Contraband To unlawfully use or possess items or substances that are listed as contraband under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:14 Covers anything under the Contraband category in Standard Operating Procedure.
\n \n \n \n
Distribution: Substances To unlawfully produce or give out substances that are controlled under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:12 Covers anything under the Controlled Substances category in Standard Operating Procedure.
Distribution: Restricted To unlawfully produce or give out equipment or weaponry that is restricted under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:16 Covers anything under the Restricted Equipment category in Standard Operating Procedure.
Distribution: Contraband To unlawfully produce or give out items or substances that are listed as contraband under the sector's active Standard Operating Procedures. Confinement: 0:20 Covers anything under the Contraband category in Standard Operating Procedure.
\n \n \n \n
Failure to Comply To refuse or fail to follow lawful orders given to you by a relevant authority. Confinement: 0:08 Includes any station authority, be it security or command, giving lawful and reasonable orders.
Resisting Arrest To attempt to flee or physically fight against a lawful arrest while outside the brig. Confinement: 0:12 This does not apply to people who had no reasonable way to know they were under arrest or were wanted by security.
Breach of Custody To attempt to escape custody while inside the brig. Confinement: 0:26, Permanent Custody, Deportation This includes attempting to break someone else out of custody.
\n \n \n \n
Trespass To enter an area you're not authorized to be in without permission. Confinement: 0:06 Permission is important, being invited into an area is not a crime.
Secure Trespass To enter a secure area you're not authorized to be in without permission. Confinement: 0:12 What is defined as a secure area is handled by the Secured Rooms section in Standard Operating Procedure.
\n \n \n \n
Coercion To threaten someone with violence or harm to get them to act in your interest. Confinement: 0:14 Akin to assault but with the intent to get the victim to do something in your favor.
Unlawful Control To unlawfully restrain, confine, or cause someone to be unable to refuse your demands without the use of threats or violence. Confinement: 0:18 Includes things like unlawful arrests, mind control, and tampering with synthetics so that they must follow your orders.
Hostage Taking To unlawfully restrain or confine someone and threaten their well being to get others to follow your demands. Confinement: 0:46, Psychiatric Custody The difference between Unlawful Control and Hostage Taking is that hostages are used to get others to comply with your demands.
\n \n \n \n
Animal Abuse To needlessly act cruel towards, endanger, harm, or torture animals considered as pets. Confinement 0:10 Livestock, varmint, and animals that are hostile to humanoids are typically recognized as non-pets, however the Captain has the final say over this.
Animal Slaughter To needlessly kill animals considered as pets. Confinement: 0:14, Psychiatric Custody Similar to animal abuse but covers intentional killing.
\n \n \n \n
Bribery To use currency or other forms of payment to get an authority to act unlawfully. Confinement: 0:08 Includes things such as attempting to bribe authorities to ignore crimes and using bribes to get a lesser punishment.
Fraud To intentionally misrepresent another person's actions. Confinement: 0:12 For things such as lying about someone's role in a crime, be it written or verbal.
Forgery To fabricate a person's signature, an official stamp, or a document. Confinement: 0:20 Intention does not matter for forgery.
Impersonation To intentionally fake your appearance or voice to represent someone or something you are not to cause confusion or to further personal interests. Confinement: 0:26 Does not cover playful impersonation, as long as they don't create genuine confusion.
